summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: States of Matter
summary_sections.content_html:
The Three States of Matter

  • Solid: Particles are tightly packed in a fixed structure. They vibrate in place but do not move freely. Solids have a definite shape and volume.
  • Liquid: Particles are close together but can move past one another. Liquids have a definite volume but take the shape of their container.
  • Gas: Particles are far apart and move freely at high speeds. Gases have no definite shape or volume and will expand to fill their container.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Intensive & Extensive Properties
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Intensive Property: A property that does NOT depend on the amount of matter (e.g., density, color, boiling point).
  • Extensive Property: A property that DOES depend on the amount of matter (e.g., mass, volume, length).
  • Density: An intensive property equal to mass divided by volume (D = m/v).
  • Color: An intensive property — a small sample and a large sample have the same color.
  • Mass: An extensive property — doubling the sample doubles the mass.
  • Volume: An extensive property — the amount of space matter occupies changes with sample size.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Heterogeneous & Homogeneous Mixtures
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Mixture: A combination of two or more substances that are NOT chemically bonded.
  • Homogeneous Mixture: A mixture with a uniform composition throughout; also called a solution (e.g., saltwater).
  • Heterogeneous Mixture: A mixture with a non-uniform composition; individual parts can be seen (e.g., salad, trail mix).
  • Solution: A homogeneous mixture where the solute is evenly distributed in the solvent.
  • Colloid: A mixture where tiny particles are dispersed but do not settle out (e.g., milk, fog).
  • Suspension: A mixture where larger particles are dispersed and eventually settle out (e.g., muddy water).

summary_sections.title: Practice Review
summary_sections.content_html:
• Q: Which of the following is a chemical property?
    A: Flammability
  • Q: Which of the following does NOT indicate a chemical change?
    A: Melting
  • Q: True or False: Chopping wood is a physical change.
    A: True
  • Q: Which property describes the ability of a substance to burn?
    A: Flammability
  • Q: Which of the following is a PHYSICAL change?
    A: Melting ice
  • Q: A compound is defined as...
    A: Two or more elements chemically combined.
  • Q: Which method is best for separating a solid from a liquid in a heterogeneous mixture?
    A: Filtration
  • Q: The Law of Conservation of Mass states that:
    A: Mass is neither created nor destroyed.
  • Q: What is the smallest unit of an element that retains its properties?
    A: Atom
  • Q: Which is a Chemical Property?
    A: Flammability

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Energy & Matter

Core Definitions

  • Energy: The ability to do work or cause change; cannot be created or destroyed (Law of Conservation of Energy).
  • Kinetic Energy: The energy of motion; increases as particles move faster.
  • Potential Energy: Stored energy based on position or composition.
  • Thermal Energy: The total kinetic energy of all particles in a substance; related to temperature.
  • Endothermic Process: A process that absorbs energy from the surroundings (feels cold).
  • Exothermic Process: A process that releases energy to the surroundings (feels hot).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Calculating pH and pOH using Log
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • pH Formula: pH = −log[H⁺], where [H⁺] is the molar concentration of hydrogen ions.
  • pOH Formula: pOH = −log[OH⁻], where [OH⁻] is the molar concentration of hydroxide ions.
  • Reverse Calculation: [H⁺] = 10⁻ᵖᴴ and [OH⁻] = 10⁻ᵖᴼᴴ.
  • Key Relationship: pH + pOH = 14; [H⁺] × [OH⁻] = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Acid & Base Properties
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Acid: A substance that produces H⁺ (hydrogen) ions in solution; tastes sour; pH below 7.
  • Base: A substance that produces OH⁻ (hydroxide) ions in solution; tastes bitter, feels slippery; pH above 7.
  • Arrhenius Definition: Acids produce H⁺ in water; bases produce OH⁻ in water.
  • Brønsted-Lowry Definition: Acids are proton (H⁺) donors; bases are proton acceptors.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Binary Acids vs Oxyacids
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Binary Acid: An acid made of hydrogen and one other nonmetal element (e.g., HCl, HBr).
  • Oxyacid: An acid containing hydrogen, oxygen, and another element (e.g., H₂SO₄, HNO₃).
  • Binary Acid Naming: Use the prefix "hydro-" + root of the element + "-ic acid" (e.g., hydrochloric acid).
  • Oxyacid Naming: If the polyatomic ion ends in "-ate," the acid ends in "-ic acid"; if "-ite," then "-ous acid."

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Naming Acids
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Naming Binary Acids: hydro- + element root + -ic acid. Example: HBr → hydrobromic acid.
  • Naming Oxyacids (-ate): Drop "-ate," add "-ic acid." Example: H₂SO₄ (sulfate → sulfuric acid).
  • Naming Oxyacids (-ite): Drop "-ite," add "-ous acid." Example: H₂SO₃ (sulfite → sulfurous acid).
  • Writing Formulas: Identify the anion, determine its charge, balance with H⁺ ions.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Identifying Acids & Bases
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Indicators: Chemicals that change color depending on pH (e.g., litmus, phenolphthalein).
  • Litmus Test: Blue litmus turns red in acid; red litmus turns blue in base.
  • Phenolphthalein: Colorless in acid, turns pink/magenta in base (pH > 8.2).
  • pH Paper: Universal indicator paper that shows a range of colors for different pH values.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Strong vs Weak Acids & Bases
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Strong Acid: Completely dissociates (ionizes) in water. Examples: HCl, HNO₃, H₂SO₄.
  • Weak Acid: Only partially dissociates in water; establishes equilibrium. Example: CH₃COOH (acetic acid).
  • Strong Base: Completely dissociates in water. Examples: NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)₂.
  • Weak Base: Only partially dissociates in water. Example: NH₃ (ammonia).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Neutralization Reactions
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Neutralization: A reaction between an acid and a base that produces water and a salt.
  • General Equation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water (HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O).
  • Titration: A lab technique to determine the concentration of an acid or base using a neutralization reaction.
  • Equivalence Point: The point in a titration where moles of acid equal moles of base.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Naming Salts from Neutralization
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Salt: An ionic compound formed from the cation of a base and the anion of an acid.
  • Naming Rule: The cation comes from the base (metal), and the anion comes from the acid.
  • From Binary Acids: The anion keeps the "-ide" ending. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl (sodium chloride).
  • From Oxyacids: The anion keeps the polyatomic name. Example: HNO₃ + KOH → KNO₃ (potassium nitrate).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Buffer Solutions
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Buffer: A solution that resists changes in pH when small amounts of acid or base are added.
  • Composition: Made of a weak acid and its conjugate base, or a weak base and its conjugate acid.
  • Buffer Capacity: The amount of acid or base a buffer can absorb before pH changes significantly.
  • Biological Example: Blood is buffered at pH 7.4 by the carbonic acid–bicarbonate system (H₂CO₃/HCO₃⁻).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: pH & pOH Scale
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • pH Scale: Measures hydrogen ion concentration; ranges from 0 (most acidic) to 14 (most basic); 7 is neutral.
  • pOH: Measures hydroxide ion concentration. pH + pOH = 14 at 25°C.
  • Acidic Solution: pH < 7 (high [H⁺], low [OH⁻]).
  • Basic Solution: pH > 7 (low [H⁺], high [OH⁻]).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Heat Conversion
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Heat (q): Energy transferred between objects due to a temperature difference, measured in joules (J) or calories (cal).
  • Joule (J): The SI unit of energy. 1 calorie = 4.184 joules.
  • Calorie (cal): The amount of energy needed to raise 1 g of water by 1°C.
  • Kilocalorie (kcal): 1 kcal = 1000 cal = 1 food Calorie (Cal).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Specific Heat
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Specific Heat (c): The amount of energy needed to raise 1 gram of a substance by 1°C.
  • Formula: q = mcΔT, where q = heat, m = mass, c = specific heat, ΔT = change in temperature.
  • Water's Specific Heat: 4.184 J/g°C — very high, which is why water heats and cools slowly.
  • Units: J/(g·°C) or cal/(g·°C).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Heat Capacity
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Heat Capacity (C): The amount of heat needed to raise the temperature of an entire object by 1°C.
  • Difference from Specific Heat: Heat capacity depends on total mass; specific heat is per gram.
  • Formula: q = CΔT, where C = heat capacity of the object.
  • Relationship: C = m × c (heat capacity = mass × specific heat).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Calorimetry
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Calorimetry: The measurement of heat changes during chemical or physical processes.
  • Calorimeter: A device used to measure heat exchange. A simple version is a coffee-cup calorimeter.
  • Principle: q_lost = −q_gained. Heat lost by a hot object equals heat gained by a cold object.
  • Bomb Calorimeter: Used for combustion reactions; measures heat at constant volume.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Enthalpy Entropy Free Energy
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Enthalpy (H): The total heat content of a system. ΔH < 0 = exothermic; ΔH > 0 = endothermic.
  • Entropy (S): A measure of disorder/randomness. ΔS > 0 means disorder increases.
  • Gibbs Free Energy (G): ΔG = ΔH − TΔS. If ΔG < 0, the reaction is spontaneous.
  • Spontaneous Reaction: Occurs without continuous outside energy input (ΔG < 0).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Hess's Law
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Hess's Law: The total enthalpy change for a reaction is the same regardless of the pathway taken.
  • Application: Add ΔH values of individual steps to find the overall ΔH of a reaction.
  • Reversing a Reaction: Changes the sign of ΔH. If forward is −100 kJ, reverse is +100 kJ.
  • Multiplying a Reaction: Multiply ΔH by the same factor. If doubled, ΔH doubles.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Nuclear Fusion and Fission
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Nuclear Fission: The splitting of a heavy nucleus into two lighter nuclei, releasing large amounts of energy.
  • Nuclear Fusion: The combining of two light nuclei into one heavier nucleus, releasing even more energy.
  • Chain Reaction: In fission, released neutrons trigger further fission events, sustaining the reaction.
  • Mass Defect: The difference between the mass of the nucleus and the sum of its individual protons and neutrons — converted to energy via E = mc².

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Alpha Beta & Gamma Decay

Core Definitions

  • Alpha Decay (α): Emission of a helium-4 nucleus (²₄He). Atomic number decreases by 2, mass number by 4.
  • Beta Decay (β): A neutron converts to a proton and emits an electron (⁰₋₁e). Atomic number increases by 1.
  • Gamma Decay (γ): Emission of high-energy electromagnetic radiation. No change in atomic or mass number.
  • Penetrating Power: α < β < γ. Alpha stopped by paper; beta by aluminum; gamma by lead/concrete.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Nuclear Reactions
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Nuclear Equation: Shows the parent nucleus, emitted particle, and daughter nucleus. Mass and atomic numbers must balance.
  • Transmutation: The conversion of one element into another through nuclear reactions.
  • Balancing Rule: Sum of mass numbers on left = sum on right; sum of atomic numbers on left = sum on right.
  • Particle Symbols: α = ⁴₂He, β = ⁰₋₁e, γ = ⁰₀γ, neutron = ¹₀n, proton = ¹₁p.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Half-Life Calculations

Core Definitions

  • Half-Life (t½): The time it takes for half of a radioactive sample to decay.
  • Formula: Remaining = Original × (½)ⁿ, where n = number of half-lives elapsed.
  • Finding n: n = total time ÷ half-life.
  • Key Feature: Half-life is constant — it does not change regardless of the amount of sample.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Applications of Nuclear Chemistry

Core Definitions

  • Nuclear Power: Fission of uranium-235 heats water to produce steam that drives turbines for electricity.
  • Medical Uses: Radioisotopes used in imaging (PET scans), cancer treatment (radiation therapy), and tracers.
  • Carbon Dating: Uses the decay of carbon-14 to determine the age of organic materials up to ~50,000 years.
  • Food Irradiation: Gamma rays kill bacteria in food to extend shelf life without making food radioactive.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Scientific Notation
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Scientific Notation: A way to express very large or very small numbers as a × 10ⁿ, where 1 ≤ a < 10.
  • Coefficient: The number 'a' in scientific notation; must be between 1 and 10.
  • Exponent: The power of 10 that indicates how many places the decimal moves.
  • Positive Exponent: Indicates a large number; decimal moves to the right (e.g., 3.0 × 10³ = 3000).
  • Negative Exponent: Indicates a small number; decimal moves to the left (e.g., 2.5 × 10⁻² = 0.025).
  • Standard Form: Writing a number without scientific notation (e.g., 45000 instead of 4.5 × 10⁴).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Significant Figures
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Significant Figures: The digits in a measurement that are known with certainty plus one estimated digit.
  • Non-zero Digits: All non-zero digits are always significant (e.g., 123 has 3 sig figs).
  • Captive Zeros: Zeros between non-zero digits are significant (e.g., 1002 has 4 sig figs).
  • Leading Zeros: Zeros before the first non-zero digit are NOT significant (e.g., 0.0045 has 2 sig figs).
  • Trailing Zeros: Zeros at the end of a number are significant only if there is a decimal point (e.g., 100. has 3 sig figs).
  • Exact Numbers: Numbers obtained by counting or by definition have unlimited significant figures.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Accuracy vs Precision

Core Definitions

  • Accuracy: How close a measurement is to the true or accepted value.
  • Precision: How close repeated measurements are to each other (reproducibility).
  • Percent Error: Measures accuracy: |experimental − accepted| / accepted × 100%.
  • High Accuracy, High Precision: Measurements are close to the true value AND close to each other.
  • High Precision, Low Accuracy: Measurements are close to each other but far from the true value.
  • Systematic Error: A consistent error that affects accuracy (e.g., a miscalibrated instrument).

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: The Metric System

Core Definitions

  • Metric System: A decimal-based system of measurement used in science; based on powers of 10.
  • Base Units: Meter (length), gram (mass), liter (volume), second (time).
  • Kilo- (k): Prefix meaning 1,000 times the base unit (1 km = 1000 m).
  • Centi- (c): Prefix meaning 1/100 of the base unit (1 cm = 0.01 m).
  • Milli- (m): Prefix meaning 1/1000 of the base unit (1 mm = 0.001 m).
  • Micro- (μ): Prefix meaning 1/1,000,000 of the base unit.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Unit Conversions
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Dimensional Analysis: A method of converting units using conversion factors to cancel unwanted units.
  • Conversion Factor: A ratio equal to 1 that relates two different units (e.g., 1 km / 1000 m).
  • Setting Up Conversions: Place the given unit in a position to cancel, and the desired unit in the answer position.
  • Multi-step Conversion: Some conversions require more than one conversion factor chained together.
  • Unit Cancellation: When a unit appears in both numerator and denominator, it cancels out.
  • Always Check Units: The final answer must have the correct desired unit to verify the conversion.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Electrons, Protons & Neutrons

Core Definitions

  • Proton: A positively charged subatomic particle found in the nucleus; defines the element (atomic number).
  • Neutron: A neutral subatomic particle found in the nucleus; contributes to atomic mass.
  • Electron: A negatively charged particle orbiting the nucleus in energy levels; very small mass.
  • Atomic Number: The number of protons in an atom; identifies the element.
  • Mass Number: The total number of protons + neutrons in the nucleus.
  • Charge Balance: In a neutral atom, the number of protons equals the number of electrons.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Atomic Structure

Core Definitions

  • Nucleus: The dense, central core of an atom containing protons and neutrons.
  • Electron Cloud: The region around the nucleus where electrons are most likely to be found.
  • Energy Levels: Regions around the nucleus where electrons orbit; numbered 1, 2, 3, etc.
  • Atomic Radius: The size of an atom measured from the nucleus to the outer electron cloud.
  • Nuclear Force: The strong force that holds protons and neutrons together in the nucleus.
  • Atom: The smallest unit of an element that retains the element's chemical properties.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Quantum Mechanical Model & Orbitals

Core Definitions

  • Quantum Mechanical Model: The modern model of the atom that treats electrons as waves and defines probability regions (orbitals).
  • Orbital: A region of space where there is a high probability of finding an electron.
  • s Orbital: Spherical-shaped orbital; each energy level has one s orbital (holds 2 electrons).
  • p Orbital: Dumbbell-shaped orbital; exists starting from the 2nd energy level (3 orbitals, holds 6 electrons).
  • d Orbital: Clover-shaped orbital; exists starting from the 3rd energy level (5 orbitals, holds 10 electrons).
  • Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle: You cannot simultaneously know both the exact position and momentum of an electron.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: EM Spectrum & Atomic Emission Spectra

Core Definitions

  • Electromagnetic Spectrum: The full range of electromagnetic radiation from radio waves to gamma rays.
  • Wavelength (λ): The distance between two consecutive wave peaks; measured in meters or nanometers.
  • Frequency (ν): The number of wave cycles per second; measured in hertz (Hz).
  • Speed of Light: c = λ × ν = 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s; wavelength and frequency are inversely related.
  • Atomic Emission Spectrum: The unique pattern of light frequencies emitted by an element's excited electrons.
  • Photon: A particle (quantum) of electromagnetic radiation; energy = hν.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Radioactivity & Stability
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Radioactivity: The spontaneous emission of radiation from an unstable nucleus.
  • Nuclear Stability: Depends on the ratio of neutrons to protons; too many or too few neutrons = unstable.
  • Band of Stability: The zone on a graph of neutrons vs. protons where stable nuclei exist.
  • Alpha Decay: Emission of an alpha particle (²₄He); reduces atomic number by 2 and mass number by 4.
  • Beta Decay: A neutron converts to a proton and emits a beta particle (electron); atomic number increases by 1.
  • Gamma Radiation: High-energy electromagnetic radiation emitted from the nucleus; no change in mass or atomic number.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Isotopes & Radioisotopes
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Isotope: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons (same protons, different mass numbers).
  • Radioisotope: An unstable isotope that undergoes radioactive decay.
  • Atomic Mass: The weighted average mass of all naturally occurring isotopes of an element.
  • Mass Number: The total number of protons and neutrons in a specific isotope.
  • Isotope Notation: Written as Element-mass number (e.g., Carbon-14) or with superscript mass number.
  • Percent Abundance: The percentage of each isotope found naturally; used to calculate atomic mass.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Element Synthesis

Core Definitions

  • Element Synthesis: The creation of new elements by bombarding target nuclei with high-speed particles.
  • Particle Accelerator: A machine that speeds up charged particles to collide with target atoms.
  • Transuranium Elements: Elements with atomic numbers greater than 92 (uranium); all are synthetic.
  • Nuclear Fusion: Combining two lighter nuclei to form a heavier one; releases enormous energy.
  • Synthetic Elements: Elements not found naturally; created in labs using nuclear reactions.
  • Super-heavy Elements: Elements with very high atomic numbers (e.g., oganesson, element 118); extremely unstable.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Atomic Theory
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Democritus: Greek philosopher who first proposed that matter is made of small, indivisible particles called 'atomos.'
  • Dalton's Atomic Theory: All matter is made of atoms; atoms of the same element are identical; atoms combine in whole-number ratios.
  • Thomson's Model: Discovered the electron; proposed the 'plum pudding' model with negative charges in positive mass.
  • Rutherford's Model: Discovered the nucleus through the gold foil experiment; most of the atom is empty space.
  • Bohr's Model: Electrons orbit the nucleus in fixed energy levels (like planets around the sun).
  • Modern Model: The quantum mechanical model; electrons exist in probability clouds (orbitals), not fixed paths.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Chemical Symbols
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Chemical Symbol: A one- or two-letter abbreviation for an element (e.g., H for hydrogen, Na for sodium).
  • First Letter: Always capitalized (e.g., C for carbon).
  • Second Letter: Always lowercase (e.g., Ca for calcium, not CA).
  • Latin-Based Symbols: Some symbols come from Latin names (Na = natrium/sodium, Fe = ferrum/iron, Au = aurum/gold).
  • Periodic Table: The organized chart listing all known elements with their symbols, atomic numbers, and masses.
  • Element vs. Compound: Elements have single symbols; compounds combine symbols (e.g., H₂O, NaCl).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Periodic Table Arrangement
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Periods: Horizontal rows on the periodic table; elements in the same period have the same number of energy levels.
  • Groups/Families: Vertical columns; elements in the same group have similar chemical properties and the same number of valence electrons.
  • Metals: Found on the left side; good conductors, malleable, ductile, shiny.
  • Nonmetals: Found on the right side; poor conductors, brittle as solids, dull.
  • Metalloids: Found along the staircase line; have properties of both metals and nonmetals (e.g., silicon).
  • Atomic Number Order: Elements are arranged by increasing atomic number from left to right.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Valence Electrons & Reactivity
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Valence Electrons: Electrons in the outermost energy level; determine an element's chemical behavior.
  • Octet Rule: Atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to have 8 valence electrons (stable).
  • Alkali Metals (Group 1): Have 1 valence electron; very reactive, easily lose 1 electron.
  • Halogens (Group 17): Have 7 valence electrons; very reactive, easily gain 1 electron.
  • Noble Gases (Group 18): Have 8 valence electrons (full outer shell); very stable and unreactive.
  • Reactivity Trends: Metals become more reactive going down a group; nonmetals become more reactive going up.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Electron Suborbitals
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Sublevel (Subshell): Subdivisions within energy levels: s, p, d, f.
  • s Sublevel: 1 orbital, holds up to 2 electrons; found in every energy level.
  • p Sublevel: 3 orbitals, holds up to 6 electrons; starts at energy level 2.
  • d Sublevel: 5 orbitals, holds up to 10 electrons; starts at energy level 3.
  • f Sublevel: 7 orbitals, holds up to 14 electrons; starts at energy level 4.
  • Aufbau Principle: Electrons fill the lowest-energy sublevel first before moving to higher ones.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Electron Configuration
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Electron Configuration: The arrangement of electrons in an atom's orbitals (e.g., 1s² 2s² 2p⁶).
  • Aufbau Principle: Electrons fill orbitals from lowest to highest energy.
  • Pauli Exclusion Principle: Each orbital can hold a maximum of 2 electrons with opposite spins.
  • Hund's Rule: Electrons fill orbitals of the same sublevel singly before pairing up.
  • Noble Gas Shorthand: Uses the preceding noble gas symbol in brackets to abbreviate (e.g., [Ne] 3s² 3p³ for phosphorus).
  • Diagonal Rule: The filling order follows a diagonal pattern: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p...

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Periodic Trends
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Atomic Radius: Increases going DOWN a group (more energy levels) and DECREASES going across a period (more protons pull electrons closer).
  • Ionization Energy: The energy required to remove an electron; INCREASES across a period and DECREASES down a group.
  • Electronegativity: An atom's ability to attract electrons in a bond; INCREASES across a period and DECREASES down a group.
  • Electron Affinity: The energy released when an atom gains an electron; generally increases across a period.
  • Metallic Character: INCREASES going down a group and DECREASES going across a period.
  • Fluorine: The most electronegative element on the periodic table.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Shielding Effect
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Shielding Effect: Inner electrons block (shield) outer electrons from the full nuclear charge.
  • Effective Nuclear Charge (Zeff): The net positive charge felt by valence electrons after shielding: Zeff = Z − S.
  • More Shielding: As you go DOWN a group, more inner electron shells increase shielding, so outer electrons are held less tightly.
  • Across a Period: Shielding stays roughly the same, but nuclear charge increases, so Zeff increases.
  • Effect on Atomic Radius: Greater shielding → larger atomic radius (electrons are farther from nucleus).
  • Effect on Ionization Energy: Greater shielding → lower ionization energy (easier to remove outer electrons).

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: VSEPR & Molecule Shapes

Core Definitions

  • VSEPR Theory: Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion — electron pairs around a central atom arrange to minimize repulsion.
  • Linear: 2 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs → 180° bond angle (e.g., CO₂).
  • Trigonal Planar: 3 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs → 120° bond angle (e.g., BF₃).
  • Tetrahedral: 4 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs → 109.5° bond angle (e.g., CH₄).
  • Bent: 2 bonding pairs, 1-2 lone pairs → less than 120° or 109.5° (e.g., H₂O).
  • Lone Pairs: Non-bonding electron pairs that take up space and compress bond angles.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Suborbital Shapes

Core Definitions

  • s Orbital Shape: Spherical; exists in every energy level.
  • p Orbital Shape: Dumbbell (figure-eight); 3 orientations along x, y, z axes.
  • d Orbital Shape: Clover-leaf (most) and donut-dumbbell; 5 orientations.
  • f Orbital Shape: Complex multi-lobed shapes; 7 orientations.
  • Orbital Size: Orbitals of the same type get larger at higher energy levels (e.g., 3s > 2s > 1s).
  • Node: A region where the probability of finding an electron is zero.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Types of Reactions
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Synthesis (Combination): Two or more reactants combine to form one product: A + B → AB.
  • Decomposition: One compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances: AB → A + B.
  • Single Replacement: One element replaces another in a compound: A + BC → AC + B.
  • Double Replacement: Ions in two compounds exchange partners: AB + CD → AD + CB.
  • Combustion: A substance reacts with oxygen, releasing energy as heat and light.
  • Precipitate: An insoluble solid formed in a double replacement reaction.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Combustion Reactions
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Combustion: A rapid reaction of a substance with oxygen that produces heat and light (fire).
  • Complete Combustion: Hydrocarbon + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O; occurs with plenty of oxygen.
  • Incomplete Combustion: Produces CO (carbon monoxide) or C (soot) instead of CO₂ due to limited oxygen.
  • Hydrocarbon: A compound containing only carbon and hydrogen (e.g., CH₄, C₃H₈).
  • Fuel: A substance that undergoes combustion to release usable energy.
  • Products of Complete Combustion: Always carbon dioxide (CO₂) and water (H₂O).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Redox Reactions
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Oxidation: Loss of electrons (or gain of oxygen); oxidation number increases.
  • Reduction: Gain of electrons (or loss of oxygen); oxidation number decreases.
  • Redox Reaction: A reaction where oxidation and reduction occur simultaneously.
  • Oxidizing Agent: The substance that is reduced (gains electrons); causes oxidation in another substance.
  • Reducing Agent: The substance that is oxidized (loses electrons); causes reduction in another substance.
  • OIL RIG: Memory aid: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain (of electrons).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Activation Energy
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Activation Energy (Ea): The minimum energy required for a chemical reaction to begin.
  • Energy Diagram: A graph showing energy changes during a reaction; Ea is the 'hill' reactants must overcome.
  • Exothermic Reaction: Products have less energy than reactants; energy is released.
  • Endothermic Reaction: Products have more energy than reactants; energy is absorbed.
  • Activated Complex: The unstable, high-energy transition state at the top of the energy barrier.
  • Catalyst: A substance that lowers activation energy without being consumed in the reaction.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Balancing Equations
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Law of Conservation of Mass: Matter cannot be created or destroyed; atoms must balance on both sides.
  • Coefficient: A number placed in front of a formula to balance atoms (e.g., 2H₂O).
  • Subscript: A number within a formula showing how many atoms of an element are in a molecule; NEVER change subscripts.
  • Balancing Steps: 1) Write the unbalanced equation. 2) Count atoms on each side. 3) Add coefficients to balance. 4) Verify.
  • Diatomic Elements: Elements that exist as pairs: H₂, N₂, O₂, F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂ (remember HONClBrIF).
  • Balanced Equation: An equation with equal numbers of each type of atom on both sides.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Reaction Rates & Catalysts

Core Definitions

  • Reaction Rate: The speed at which reactants are converted to products.
  • Collision Theory: Particles must collide with enough energy and proper orientation to react.
  • Temperature Effect: Increasing temperature increases particle speed and collision frequency → faster rate.
  • Concentration Effect: Higher concentration means more particles → more collisions → faster rate.
  • Surface Area Effect: Smaller particles (greater surface area) react faster.
  • Catalyst: Speeds up a reaction by lowering activation energy; is not consumed.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Chemical Equilibrium & Le Chatelier's Principle

Core Definitions

  • Chemical Equilibrium: A state where the forward and reverse reaction rates are equal; concentrations remain constant.
  • Reversible Reaction: A reaction that can proceed in both forward and reverse directions (⇌).
  • Le Chatelier's Principle: If a stress is applied to a system at equilibrium, the system shifts to relieve the stress.
  • Adding Reactant: Shifts equilibrium toward products (forward).
  • Increasing Temperature: Shifts equilibrium in the endothermic direction.
  • Changing Pressure: Increasing pressure shifts toward the side with fewer moles of gas.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Writing Correct Formulas

Core Definitions

  • Chemical Formula: Uses element symbols and subscripts to show the composition of a compound (e.g., H₂O).
  • Cation: A positively charged ion formed when an atom loses electrons.
  • Anion: A negatively charged ion formed when an atom gains electrons.
  • Criss-Cross Method: Use the charge of each ion as the subscript of the other to write formulas.
  • Polyatomic Ion: A group of atoms bonded together that carry a charge (e.g., SO₄²⁻, NH₄⁺).
  • Neutral Compound Rule: The total positive charge must equal the total negative charge in a formula.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Molar Mass & Molecular Mass
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Molar Mass: The mass of one mole of a substance in grams per mole (g/mol); equals atomic mass from periodic table.
  • Molecular Mass: The sum of the atomic masses of all atoms in a molecular formula.
  • Formula Mass: The sum of atomic masses for ionic compounds.
  • Calculating Molar Mass: Add up the atomic masses of each element multiplied by their subscript.
  • Example: H₂O: 2(1.01) + 16.00 = 18.02 g/mol.
  • Units: Molar mass is expressed in g/mol; molecular mass in amu (atomic mass units).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Avogadro's Number
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Avogadro's Number: 6.022 × 10²³ — the number of particles (atoms, molecules, ions) in one mole.
  • Mole: The SI unit for amount of substance; 1 mole = 6.022 × 10²³ particles.
  • Mole-to-Particles: Multiply moles × 6.022 × 10²³ to find the number of particles.
  • Particles-to-Moles: Divide number of particles by 6.022 × 10²³ to find moles.
  • Amedeo Avogadro: Italian scientist who contributed to molecular theory; the number is named after him.
  • Universal Counting Unit: A mole relates microscopic particles to macroscopic measurable amounts.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Molar Conversions

Core Definitions

  • Moles to Grams: Multiply moles × molar mass (g/mol).
  • Grams to Moles: Divide grams by molar mass (g/mol).
  • Moles to Particles: Multiply moles × 6.022 × 10²³.
  • Particles to Moles: Divide number of particles by 6.022 × 10²³.
  • Moles to Liters (Gas at STP): Multiply moles × 22.4 L/mol.
  • Conversion Map: Particles ↔ Moles ↔ Grams; Moles ↔ Liters (gas at STP).

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Empirical vs Molecular Formulas

Core Definitions

  • Empirical Formula: The simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound (e.g., CH₂O).
  • Molecular Formula: The actual number of atoms of each element in a molecule (e.g., C₆H₁₂O₆).
  • Finding Empirical Formula: Convert percentages to grams → to moles → divide by smallest mole value → round to whole numbers.
  • Relationship: Molecular formula = n × empirical formula, where n = molar mass / empirical mass.
  • Percent Composition: The percentage by mass of each element in a compound.
  • Example: Glucose: empirical = CH₂O (30 g/mol), molecular = C₆H₁₂O₆ (180 g/mol), n = 6.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Limiting Reagents
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Limiting Reagent: The reactant that is completely consumed first and determines the maximum amount of product formed.
  • Excess Reagent: The reactant that is left over after the reaction is complete.
  • Finding the Limiting Reagent: Convert both reactants to moles of product; the one that produces LESS is the limiting reagent.
  • Stoichiometry: Using mole ratios from balanced equations to calculate amounts of reactants/products.
  • Theoretical Yield: The maximum amount of product calculated from the limiting reagent.
  • Analogy: Like a sandwich recipe: if you run out of bread before cheese, bread is the limiting reagent.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Stoichiometric Calculations
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Stoichiometry: The calculation of quantities in chemical reactions using mole ratios from balanced equations.
  • Mole Ratio: The ratio of moles of one substance to another from the coefficients of a balanced equation.
  • Steps: 1) Balance equation. 2) Convert given to moles. 3) Use mole ratio. 4) Convert to desired unit.
  • Mass-to-Mass: Given grams → moles (÷ molar mass) → mole ratio → moles → grams (× molar mass).
  • Volume-to-Volume (Gas): At STP, mole ratios equal volume ratios for gases.
  • Balanced Equation is Key: All stoichiometric calculations begin with a correctly balanced equation.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Percent Yield
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Actual Yield: The amount of product actually obtained from an experiment.
  • Theoretical Yield: The maximum amount of product calculated from stoichiometry (from limiting reagent).
  • Percent Yield: (Actual yield / Theoretical yield) × 100%.
  • Why Less Than 100%: Side reactions, incomplete reactions, loss during transfer, or impurities.
  • 100% Yield: Means the actual yield equals the theoretical yield (rare in practice).
  • Greater Than 100%: Usually indicates experimental error (e.g., impurities adding mass).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Monatomic & Diatomic Gases
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Monatomic Gas: A gas made of single atoms (e.g., noble gases: He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn).
  • Diatomic Gas: A gas made of molecules with two atoms bonded together.
  • Seven Diatomic Elements: H₂, N₂, O₂, F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂ — remember HONClBrIF.
  • Noble Gases: Monatomic; full outer shells make them stable and unreactive.
  • Diatomic in Equations: Always write diatomic elements as molecules (e.g., O₂, not O) in chemical equations.
  • Most Abundant Gas: Nitrogen (N₂) makes up about 78% of Earth's atmosphere.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Pressure & Standard Atmosphere

Core Definitions

  • Pressure: Force per unit area exerted by gas particles colliding with container walls.
  • Standard Atmosphere (atm): 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg = 760 torr.
  • Pascal (Pa): The SI unit of pressure; 1 kPa = 1000 Pa.
  • Barometer: An instrument that measures atmospheric pressure.
  • Manometer: An instrument that measures the pressure of a gas in a container.
  • STP: Standard Temperature and Pressure: 0°C (273 K) and 1 atm.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Kinetic Molecular Theory
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • KMT Assumption 1: Gas particles are in constant, random, straight-line motion.
  • KMT Assumption 2: Gas particles have negligible volume compared to the container.
  • KMT Assumption 3: There are no attractive or repulsive forces between gas particles.
  • KMT Assumption 4: Collisions between gas particles are perfectly elastic (no energy lost).
  • KMT Assumption 5: The average kinetic energy is proportional to absolute temperature (K).
  • Ideal Gas: A hypothetical gas that perfectly follows all KMT assumptions.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Boyle's Law
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Boyle's Law: At constant temperature, pressure and volume are inversely proportional: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂.
  • Inverse Relationship: As pressure increases, volume decreases (and vice versa).
  • Constant Temperature: Boyle's Law only applies when temperature does not change.
  • Example: Squeezing a balloon: increased pressure → decreased volume.
  • Graph: P vs. V graph is a downward curve (hyperbola).
  • Units: Pressure and volume can use any units as long as they match on both sides.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Charles' Law
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Charles' Law: At constant pressure, volume and temperature are directly proportional: V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂.
  • Direct Relationship: As temperature increases, volume increases (and vice versa).
  • Constant Pressure: Charles' Law only applies when pressure does not change.
  • Temperature in Kelvin: Always use Kelvin for gas law calculations (K = °C + 273).
  • Absolute Zero: 0 K (−273°C); theoretically, gas volume would be zero.
  • Example: A hot air balloon rises because heated air expands (greater volume, same pressure).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Gay-Lussac's Law
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Gay-Lussac's Law: At constant volume, pressure and temperature are directly proportional: P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂.
  • Direct Relationship: As temperature increases, pressure increases (and vice versa).
  • Constant Volume: Applies to rigid containers where volume cannot change.
  • Temperature in Kelvin: Always use Kelvin for gas law calculations.
  • Example: A tire's pressure increases on a hot day (same volume, higher temperature).
  • Pressure Cooker: Sealed container: increasing temperature raises pressure, cooking food faster.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Combined Gas Law
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Combined Gas Law: Combines Boyle's, Charles', and Gay-Lussac's: P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂.
  • When to Use: When pressure, volume, AND temperature all change (nothing held constant except moles).
  • Special Cases: Hold T constant → Boyle's; Hold P constant → Charles'; Hold V constant → Gay-Lussac's.
  • Temperature: Must always be in Kelvin.
  • Solving: Identify known values, solve for the unknown variable algebraically.
  • Units: Pressure and volume units must be consistent on both sides.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Ideal Gas Law
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Ideal Gas Law: PV = nRT; relates pressure, volume, moles, and temperature.
  • R (Gas Constant): 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K) or 8.314 J/(mol·K).
  • n: The number of moles of gas.
  • When to Use: When you need to find P, V, n, or T and have the other three values.
  • Ideal vs. Real: Ideal gases perfectly follow PV = nRT; real gases deviate at high P and low T.
  • Units Must Match R: If R = 0.0821, use atm, L, mol, and K.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Real Gases & Deviations

Core Definitions

  • Real Gases: Actual gases that deviate from ideal behavior, especially at high pressure and low temperature.
  • Intermolecular Forces: Real gas particles DO attract each other, unlike ideal gas assumptions.
  • Particle Volume: Real gas particles DO occupy space, unlike the ideal assumption of negligible volume.
  • High Pressure: Particles are forced close together; volume and intermolecular forces become significant.
  • Low Temperature: Particles move slowly; attractive forces have greater effect, causing deviation.
  • Most Ideal Behavior: Gases behave most ideally at HIGH temperature and LOW pressure.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Solution Nomenclature

Core Definitions

  • Solution: A homogeneous mixture of two or more substances evenly distributed at the molecular level.
  • Solute: The substance that is dissolved in the solution (usually present in smaller amount).
  • Solvent: The substance that does the dissolving (usually present in larger amount).
  • Aqueous Solution: A solution where water is the solvent, denoted by (aq) in chemical equations.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Concentration
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Concentration: A measure of how much solute is dissolved in a given amount of solution.
  • Concentrated Solution: Contains a large amount of solute relative to solvent.
  • Dilute Solution: Contains a small amount of solute relative to solvent.
  • Percent Concentration: (mass of solute / mass of solution) × 100%.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Dilution
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Dilution: The process of reducing the concentration of a solution by adding more solvent.
  • Dilution Equation: M₁V₁ = M₂V₂, where M is molarity and V is volume.
  • Stock Solution: A concentrated solution that is diluted to make a less concentrated solution.
  • Key Principle: The amount of solute stays the same during dilution — only solvent is added.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Molarity
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Molarity (M): The number of moles of solute per liter of solution. M = mol / L.
  • Mole: 6.022 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's number).
  • Standard Solution: A solution with a precisely known molarity.
  • Volumetric Flask: Glassware used to prepare solutions of exact molarity.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Solution Types

Core Definitions

  • Saturated Solution: Contains the maximum amount of dissolved solute at a given temperature.
  • Unsaturated Solution: Contains less than the maximum amount of solute — more can dissolve.
  • Supersaturated Solution: Contains more dissolved solute than a saturated solution; unstable.
  • Solubility: The maximum amount of solute that dissolves in a given amount of solvent at a specific temperature.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Factors Affecting Solubility
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Like Dissolves Like: Polar solvents dissolve polar/ionic solutes; nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes.
  • Temperature Effect: Increasing temperature usually increases solubility of solids but decreases solubility of gases.
  • Pressure Effect: Increasing pressure increases solubility of gases (Henry's Law) but has little effect on solids/liquids.
  • Surface Area & Agitation: Crushing solute and stirring increases the rate of dissolving (not solubility itself).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Colligative Properties
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Colligative Properties: Properties that depend on the number of solute particles, not their identity.
  • Boiling Point Elevation: Adding solute raises the boiling point of a solution above that of the pure solvent.
  • Freezing Point Depression: Adding solute lowers the freezing point of a solution below that of the pure solvent.
  • Osmotic Pressure: Pressure required to prevent osmosis across a semipermeable membrane.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Solubility Curves
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Definitions

  • Solubility Curve: A graph showing the relationship between solubility of a substance and temperature.
  • Reading the Curve: Points on the line = saturated; below = unsaturated; above = supersaturated.
  • Endothermic Dissolving: Most solid solutes show increasing solubility with temperature (curve slopes up).
  • Gas Solubility: Gas curves slope downward — gases become less soluble as temperature increases.
